lines: The shark comes out of the murk with its mouth already open, teeth catching the last of the light. You turn, feet sunk deep in silt, and shove your weapon into the rush before it reaches your chest.

Bioluminescence flares along its flank as it banks hard through the flooded archway. The current drags at your armor, cold and insistent. You brace, plant your feet, and time the thrust for the doorway.

It circles once, twice, a big pale shape cutting slow arcs through the drowned hall, patient and sure of itself. Then it stops circling. You do not wait for a third pass; you step forward and strike first.
Cold floods the temple corridor as the shark surges from a collapsed archway, scattering scrapes of old stone past your shoulders. You roll aside on the slick floor and swing hard before it can turn.
A single mouthful could end this fight, and you know it well. So you keep your feet planted, your grip sure and steady, and you meet the shark's rush with the sharpened edge instead of your fear.

The current churns green and silver as the giant shark thrashes past, close enough that you feel the wake against your skin. You steady your breathing, count the beat, and strike hard on the next pass.

Bruised stone columns line the flooded hall where the shark waits, patient as the dark itself, testing your nerve. You step forward anyway, weapon low and steady, and refuse to be the one who turns first.

It hits like a wall of muscle and cold water, driving the breath from your chest. You stagger, catch your footing on the slick floor, and answer with a quick thrust before the current pulls you apart.

Drifting motes of pale light mark where the shark last struck the ancient stone. You watch the gloom for the telltale ripple of its approach, and when it finally comes, you are already moving to meet it.

The temple floor tilts beneath drowned rubble as the shark rounds the broken pillar, hunting for an opening. You plant your back foot in the silt, breathe once, slow and even, and hold your ground firm.

Its shadow slides across the sunken tile faster than any animal its size should move, silent and sure. You do not flinch or step back. You time your strike for the instant it commits fully to the lunge.

Grit and old bone drift in slow spirals where the shark's tail cuts through the water, stirring the murk. You grip tighter, keep your eyes on the gleam of its teeth, and wait patiently for your moment.
A crescendo of bubbles marks the shark's charge before you even see its shape emerge. You brace hard against the pulling current, angle your blade low, and meet the sudden rush of teeth head on.
The cold here has real weight, pressing steady at your ribs, but you keep your stance low and your eyes fixed on the drowned archway where the shark waits somewhere in the dark to strike again.
It thrashes sideways through a curtain of silt, jaws wide and hungry, and you throw yourself under its reach at the last second, driving your weapon up hard into the open water above you.

Bioluminescent moss lights the shark's old scars as it turns slowly for another pass through the ruined hall. You count three heartbeats, steady your grip, then step into its path instead of away from it.

The current shudders as something enormous moves just past the edge of the light, unseen but felt. You raise your weapon, plant your feet deep in the muck, and wait for the shark to finally show itself.
Its teeth flash pale in the gloom, closing fast through the drowned corridor. You twist aside at the very last instant, feel the rush of cold water on your skin, and strike hard as it passes you by.

Stone flakes drift down from the cracked ceiling every time the shark slams a pillar while chasing you through the ruin. You use the cover of the falling debris, circle wide, and strike from its blind side.

The drowned hall goes dark as the shark's huge bulk blocks the faint glow from above. You do not wait for your eyes to adjust to the sudden gloom; you strike toward where its heartbeat should be beating.

A low groan of shifting rock warns you an instant before the shark bursts clean through the rubble wall. You set your stance low, breathe through the biting cold, and meet it with your steel raised high.

It circles the sunken altar once more, unhurried and patient, testing your nerve in the gathering dark. You refuse to give any ground, and when it finally lunges you are already stepping into the strike.

The current carries a strange cold tang past your face as the shark closes in fast through the ruined hall. You grip your weapon tighter, watch the churning murk, and hold perfectly steady as it nears.

Its fin cuts a straight line through the drifting silt, fast and sure of its target. You brace hard against the crumbling temple wall, time your breath carefully, and strike the instant it finally turns.

A shudder runs through the flooded corridor as the shark slams the far wall, testing your exact position in the dark. You use that single moment to close the distance fast and strike before it can recover.

The gloom parts and rows of teeth fill it, closer than you ever expected them to be. You throw yourself hard to the side, boots skidding on the slick stone floor, and answer with one quick, desperate thrust.

Cold currents twist slowly around the broken columns as the shark stalks the length of the drowned hall. You keep your back pressed to solid stone, weapon held ready, and wait for it to overextend badly.
It slams the shattered doorway while hunting you, sending a cloud of stone dust billowing out through the water. You use the cover of the cloud and strike hard before it can even see you coming.

The shark's huge shadow slides overhead, blotting out the faint glow that filters down from somewhere above. You crouch low against the rubble, brace your feet, and drive upward the instant it finally dives.

Something enormous stirs the silt just ahead of you in the dark, and you know before you even see it that the shark has already found you first. You raise your weapon high and hold your ground steady.

A single scrape of teeth against old stone tells you it just barely missed you in the dark. You do not celebrate for even a moment; you turn, plant your feet, and meet its next pass with cold steel raised.

The drowned hall hums with the shark's steady approach, water pressure building hard against your chest. You steady your grip on the weapon and wait calmly for the strike to finally come to you instead.
It surges past close enough to touch, jaws wide open and pale in the gloom. You duck hard under the rush, feel the current drag roughly at your armor, and strike fast as its body finally turns away.

Broken pillars mark where the shark has hunted travelers before, stone scraped raw by countless old bites. You step carefully past the wreckage, weapon held ready, refusing to become its next easy mark.

The cold bites deeper the closer you get to the sunken altar, and so does the shark's patience finally running thin. You brace low, breathe once, and meet its sudden rush of teeth head on without flinching.

A crescendo of bubbles rises as the shark circles the sunken statue slowly, clearly sizing you up for its next move. You do not wait patiently for it to decide; you step forward and strike first instead.
Its teeth catch the faint light above like scattered old coin, gleaming through the murk. You keep your footing steady on the silted floor and time your thrust for the narrow gap between its passes.
The old temple shudders hard as the shark rams a fallen column right near you, testing the water for weakness. You use the tremor to close in fast and strike before it can fully recover its balance.

A dozen old scars mark the pillars here, scraped raw by countless failed hunts over the years. You refuse to become the next one, planting your feet firm and raising your weapon as the shark turns back.

It breaks from the gloom with a mouthful of open water and jaws already closing fast around you. You twist aside hard, boots slipping on the wet stone, and answer with one fast, sure, desperate strike.
The current goes eerily still just before the shark strikes, a calm you have learned to fear more than the charge itself. You brace hard and meet the sudden rush the very instant it finally breaks.
A groaning creak of ancient stone warns you the shark has just slammed into the far wall somewhere close. You use the sudden noise to reposition quietly and strike hard from its unguarded blind side.

Its huge bulk fills the drowned doorway completely, blocking your only way forward through the ruin. You set your stance low, breathe through the biting cold, and drive your weapon straight toward it.

The shark's fin slices the murk in a wide, patient arc, circling somewhere just out of sight. You hold steady, watching the gap in the current where its shape must eventually pass close enough to strike.

Cold water presses hard at your chest as the shark closes the distance fast through the ruined hall. You brace against a broken pillar and strike the instant its huge shadow finally overtakes your own.

A shudder of collapsed rubble marks its last violent strike near the sunken altar behind you. You step carefully over the debris, weapon held steady, and refuse to let the shark choose the ground this time.
It turns once more slowly in the gloom, its teeth catching stray light from somewhere far above. You count the beat of your own racing heart, plant your feet firm, and step calmly into its path.
The drowned hall narrows sharply here, forcing the shark into a single straight run directly at you. You brace low, time your breath carefully, and meet the sudden charge of teeth and muscle head on.

Something vast stirs beyond the broken archway ahead, and the water itself seems to hold its breath. You raise your weapon high, steady your stance, and wait patiently for the shark to finally commit.
Its teeth flash once bright in the gloom before vanishing back into the murk just as fast. You do not relax your guard for even a second; you keep your stance and watch closely for the next pass.
The current drags loose silt slowly across your boots as the shark circles closer through the ruin. You brace, breathe steady, and hold your ground firmly until it finally decides to strike at last.
A low rumble through the flooded stone tells you the shark just slammed hard into the ceiling above. You duck the sudden falling debris and strike fast as its huge body turns back toward you again.

The shark comes at you out of the murk with its whole body moving faster than the current should allow, teeth flashing pale in the last of the light. You plant your feet in the silt, angle your weapon low, and drive it forward the instant the shadow fills the drowned doorway, feeling the impact shudder up your arm.

Bioluminescence flares along its flank as it banks hard through the flooded archway, scattering motes of pale light across the broken tile. The current drags at your armor, cold and insistent, threatening to pull your footing loose. You brace against a fallen column, time your breath, and meet its charge with steel raised high.

It circles the sunken altar once, twice, a big pale shape cutting slow patient arcs through the drowned hall, sizing you up the whole time. Then it stops circling entirely, and the water itself seems to go still. You do not wait for a third pass; you step forward through the cold and strike first, before it can decide anything for you.

Cold floods the temple corridor as the shark surges from a collapsed archway, scattering old stone scrapes past your shoulders and clouding the water with grit. You roll aside on the slick floor, catch your balance against a broken pillar, and swing hard before it can turn back for another pass at your exposed side.

A single mouthful could end this fight, and you know that better than anyone standing in this drowned hall. So you keep your feet planted deep in the silt, your grip sure and steady despite the cold, and you meet the shark's sudden rush with the sharpened edge of your weapon instead of your rising fear.

The current churns green and silver as the giant shark thrashes past close enough that you feel the wake drag hard against your skin. You steady your breathing, count the beat of your own racing heart, and strike hard on the very next pass, driving your weapon into the gap it leaves as it turns.

Bruised stone columns line the flooded hall where the shark waits, patient as the surrounding dark, testing your nerve with slow, deliberate circles. You step forward anyway, weapon held low and steady, refusing to be the one who breaks first and turns to run through the drowned ruin behind you.

It hits like a wall of muscle and freezing water, driving the breath clean from your chest and nearly knocking your weapon loose. You stagger hard, catch your footing on the slick, silted floor, and answer with a quick desperate thrust before the current can pull the two of you apart entirely.

Drifting motes of pale light mark where the shark last struck against the ancient carved stone, scattering bioluminescent dust through the water. You watch the gloom closely for the telltale ripple of its next approach, and when it finally comes rushing toward you, you are already moving to meet it head on.

The temple floor tilts sharply beneath drowned rubble as the shark rounds a broken pillar, hunting for any opening in your guard. You plant your back foot firm in the silt, breathe once, slow and even despite the cold pressing at your ribs, and hold your ground steady against the coming rush.

Its shadow slides across the sunken tile faster than any animal its size should reasonably move, silent and terribly sure of itself. You do not flinch or step back even an inch. You time your strike carefully for the exact instant it commits fully to the lunge, driving your weapon home before it can veer away.

Grit and old bone drift in slow spirals where the shark's massive tail cuts hard through the churning water, stirring the murk into a pale cloud. You grip your weapon tighter, keep your eyes fixed on the gleam of its teeth, and wait with real patience for the perfect moment to strike back.

A crescendo of bubbles marks the shark's charge before you even see its shape emerge fully from the gloom ahead. You brace hard against the pulling current, angle your blade low and steady, and meet the sudden rush of teeth and muscle head on, feeling the impact rattle straight through your bones.

The cold here has real, physical weight, pressing steady at your ribs with every passing second, but you keep your stance low regardless. Your eyes stay fixed on the drowned archway where the shark waits somewhere unseen in the dark, patient and hungry, ready to strike again the moment you relax your guard.

It thrashes sideways through a sudden curtain of silt, jaws wide open and hungry, and you throw yourself hard under its reach at the very last second. Driving your weapon up fast into the open water above you, you feel the blade connect solidly just as the current drags you both apart again.

Bioluminescent moss lights the shark's countless old scars as it turns slowly for another pass through the ruined, flooded hall. You count three careful heartbeats, steady your grip on the weapon, then step deliberately into its path instead of retreating away from the coming rush of teeth.

The current shudders hard as something enormous moves just past the edge of the failing light, unseen but unmistakably felt through the water pressing against your skin. You raise your weapon high, plant your feet deep in the muck below, and wait tensely for the shark to finally show itself completely.

Its teeth flash pale and sudden in the gloom, closing fast through the narrow drowned corridor ahead of you. You twist aside at the very last instant, feeling the cold rush of water drag hard across your skin, and strike hard as its huge body finally passes close enough to reach.

Stone flakes drift steadily down from the cracked ceiling every single time the shark slams a pillar while chasing you through the ruin. You use the cover of the falling debris cloud, circle wide through the murky water, and strike hard and fast from its unguarded blind side while it recovers.

The drowned hall goes completely dark as the shark's huge bulk blocks the last faint glow filtering down from somewhere far above. You do not wait for your eyes to adjust to the sudden gloom around you; instead you strike hard toward where its racing heartbeat should be, trusting your instincts over your sight.

A low groan of shifting rock warns you an instant before the shark bursts clean through a wall of loose rubble nearby. You set your stance low and firm, breathe hard through the biting cold pressing at your chest, and meet it with your steel raised high, ready for the impact.

It circles the sunken altar once more, unhurried and deeply patient, testing your nerve in the gathering dark around the ruined temple. You refuse to give any ground at all, and when it finally lunges forward you are already stepping into the strike, weapon leading the way.

The current carries a strange, cold tang past your face as the shark closes the distance fast through the flooded, ruined hall. You grip your weapon tighter still, watch the churning murk closely for any sign of movement, and hold perfectly steady as its huge shape finally nears within striking range.

Its fin cuts a straight, deliberate line through the drifting silt, fast and terribly sure of its intended target. You brace hard against the crumbling temple wall behind you, time your breath carefully, and strike the very instant it finally turns to commit to its charge.

A shudder runs hard through the flooded corridor as the shark slams the far wall, testing your exact position somewhere in the dark water. You use that single brief moment to close the distance fast and strike before it can fully recover its bearings and turn back toward you.

The gloom parts suddenly and rows of teeth fill it, closer than you ever expected them to actually be in that instant. You throw yourself hard to the side, boots skidding wildly on the slick stone floor beneath you, and answer with one quick, desperate thrust straight into its passing flank.

Cold currents twist slowly around the broken columns as the shark stalks the full length of the drowned, ruined hall ahead. You keep your back pressed firm against solid stone, weapon held steady and ready, and wait patiently for it to overextend badly on its next reckless pass.

It slams the shattered doorway hard while hunting you relentlessly, sending a huge cloud of stone dust billowing out through the murky water. You use the cover of that drifting cloud and strike hard and fast before it can even see you closing the distance through the gloom.

The shark's huge shadow slides directly overhead, blotting out the faint glow that filters weakly down from somewhere far above the ruin. You crouch low against the scattered rubble, brace your feet firmly, and drive upward hard the instant it finally commits to the dive.

Something enormous stirs the silt just ahead of you somewhere in the dark water, and you know before you even see it clearly that the shark has already found you first. You raise your weapon high without hesitation and hold your ground steady, refusing to retreat one single step.

A single scrape of teeth against old, carved stone tells you it just barely missed striking you in the surrounding dark. You do not celebrate for even a moment; instead you turn fast, plant your feet firm, and meet its very next pass with cold, ready steel raised high.

The drowned hall hums steadily with the shark's relentless approach, water pressure building hard and heavy against your chest with every passing second. You steady your grip on the weapon and wait calmly for the strike to finally come to you, trusting your timing over blind panic.

The descent into the sunken temple takes you past columns wrapped in pale, glowing moss, the only light in a world gone completely black beyond your reach. Cold water presses at your chest with every step deeper, and the silence has a texture to it, thick and expectant, like the whole drowned building is holding its breath and waiting for you to make the first mistake. Then the water shudders, and a shape the size of a fallen tree cuts across the flooded hall ahead, jaws already opening. You plant your feet in the silt, raise your weapon, and meet the giant shark's first rush with everything you have, feeling the impact rattle up through your arms and into your chest.

You have fought in cold water before, but nothing prepares you for the moment the shark's shadow finally swallows the last of the light above the drowned altar. Its bulk fills the corridor completely, blocking any hope of retreat, and for one long breath you simply stand there, weapon raised, waiting to see which way it will finally turn. It chooses the direct approach, surging forward with a speed that seems impossible for something so large. You throw yourself sideways into the current, boots dragging against slick, ancient tile, and answer its passing rush with a strike aimed for the softer flesh just behind its jaw.

The temple corridor narrows here, forcing anything that hunts inside it into a single straight line, and you use that fact deliberately, backing into the tightest stretch you can find. When the giant shark finally comes for you, it has no room left to circle or feint, only to charge straight down the throat of the passage toward where you stand waiting. You brace against a fallen column, time your breath to the rhythm of the current, and meet the charge head on rather than dodge it. The impact staggers you hard, driving you back a full step, but your blade finds purchase in that same instant, and steaming blood clouds the water between you both.

Bioluminescent fungus lights the drowned hall in soft, shifting patches of blue and green, throwing strange shadows across broken statues that once guarded this place. You move carefully between them, weapon ready, aware that the giant shark could be resting in any one of the deeper pools of darkness between the pale light. It reveals itself with a sudden surge from behind a collapsed pillar, jaws already closing on the space you occupied a heartbeat earlier. You are already moving, twisting hard against the drag of the current, and your counterstrike lands solid as its momentum carries it past you into the open water beyond.

The current here runs strange, curling in slow spirals around broken pillars that have not felt open air in centuries, stirring silt into pale, drifting clouds. You watch the water closely, reading its subtle motion for any sign of the giant shark's approach before your own eyes can catch the shape itself. The telltale shudder comes at last, and you brace hard against a nearby column just as the enormous shadow finally breaks from the gloom. Its rush is fast and sure, but you have already committed to your stance, and you meet the charge with steel raised, feeling the shock of impact travel straight through your bones.

Deeper into the drowned temple, the water grows colder still, pressing at your ribs with a weight that feels almost physical, almost deliberate. Somewhere ahead, past a curtain of drifting silt, something vast stirs the murk, and you know without needing to see it clearly that the giant shark has already caught your scent. You do not wait for it to choose the moment of attack. Instead you push forward through the cold current, weapon leading the way, and when the shark finally breaks from cover with jaws wide, you are already committed to the strike, meeting its rush before it can fully close the gap between you.

A dozen scars mark the broken columns of this hall, souvenirs of hunts that ended badly for whatever came before you, scraped deep into ancient stone by countless failed strikes. You study them for only a moment before turning your attention back to the drowned corridor ahead, weapon ready, refusing to become the next set of marks on the wall. The giant shark announces itself with a slam against the far wall, testing the water for your exact position with practiced patience. You use that single moment of noise to reposition fast, closing the distance while its attention is elsewhere, and strike hard from the blind side it never expected you to reach.

The sunken altar sits at the heart of the drowned hall, surrounded by broken statues worn smooth by centuries of slow current, and something about the stillness here feels deliberately wrong. You approach carefully anyway, weapon ready, watching the shifting shadows between the pale glow of scattered moss for any sign of movement. The giant shark erupts from behind the altar itself, closer than you thought possible, jaws already wide and closing fast. You throw yourself hard to the side, feeling the rush of displaced water drag at your armor, and answer its passing charge with a fast, desperate strike of your own.

Cold water floods the corridor ahead of you in slow, deliberate waves, each one a fraction colder than the last, marking the giant shark's approach long before you can see its shape in the gloom. You brace against a broken pillar, steady your breathing, and wait with real patience for the moment it finally commits. When it comes, the charge is sudden and overwhelming, closing the distance faster than you expected despite your preparation. You meet it anyway, driving your weapon forward into the rush of teeth and muscle, and the impact staggers you both, sending clouds of silt billowing through the water around you.

The drowned hall stretches long and dark ahead of you, its ceiling lost somewhere above in shadow, its floor buried under centuries of silt and broken stone. You move carefully, weapon ready, listening past the constant pressure of the water for any sign of the giant shark's approach through the gloom. It finds you first anyway, surging from a collapsed archway with a speed that closes the gap before you can fully react. You twist hard against the current, feeling your boots skid on the slick tile beneath you, and drive your weapon up into its exposed flank as it passes close enough to touch.

Somewhere past the broken archway, the water itself seems to hold still, an unnatural calm you have learned to distrust more than any obvious threat. You raise your weapon and wait, feet planted firm in the silt, watching the darkness for the shape you know is already circling somewhere just out of sight. The stillness breaks all at once, and the giant shark surges forward with jaws wide, closing the distance in a single powerful burst. You meet the charge head on, bracing hard against the impact, and your blade finds its mark just as the current threatens to sweep you both away entirely.

The temple ceiling groans overhead, ancient stone shifting under the weight of centuries and the slow pressure of the crushing dark water above. You press on regardless, weapon ready, aware that the giant shark somewhere ahead has likely already heard every step you have taken through this drowned ruin. It confirms your fear a moment later, bursting from a curtain of silt with a speed that seems impossible given its size. You throw yourself sideways hard, feeling the rush of its passing jaws graze close enough to feel, and answer with a fast strike before it can circle back for another attempt.

You have learned to read the current here, the way it shifts and drags differently when something large moves through the drowned hall ahead of you. Right now it pulls strange, curling back on itself near a collapsed pillar, and you know before you even see the shape that the giant shark is closer than it should be. You brace against the nearest solid stone, weapon raised, and wait for the moment it finally reveals itself. When it does, jaws wide and closing fast, you meet the charge without hesitation, driving your blade home just as the impact staggers you both back through the churning water.

The drowned corridor narrows sharply just ahead, forcing anything large enough to hunt inside it into a single straight line with no room to maneuver. You retreat into that tight passage deliberately, weapon ready, choosing the ground carefully before the giant shark can choose it for you instead. It comes anyway, charging straight down the corridor with a speed that leaves no room for doubt about its intentions or its hunger. You brace low, time your breath, and meet the rush head on, feeling the full weight of the impact travel up through your arms as your blade finally finds purchase in its passing flank.

Broken statues line the flooded hall here, worn faceless by centuries of slow current, their outstretched arms pointing toward a sunken altar you have not yet reached. Something vast stirs beyond them in the gloom, and you raise your weapon, certain the giant shark has already marked your position in the drowned dark. It breaks cover a moment later, cutting between two fallen statues with terrible speed, jaws already wide. You twist aside at the last possible instant, boots slipping on centuries of silt, and answer its passing rush with a fast, sure strike aimed for the softer flesh behind its jaw.

The cold in this part of the temple has real weight, pressing steady against your ribs with every breath, colder even than the water further back near the entrance. You press forward anyway, weapon ready, watching the gloom ahead for any sign of the giant shark you know is somewhere close, circling patiently. The attack comes from below, a sudden surge up through the silt that nearly catches you flat-footed before you can react. You throw yourself back hard, feeling the rush of displaced water drag at your legs, and strike down fast as its huge shape passes beneath you toward the open hall beyond.

Ahead, the drowned corridor opens into a wide gallery ringed by broken pillars, each one scarred deep by old bites and older failed strikes from hunts that came before your own. You move carefully into the open space, weapon ready, aware that the extra room here favors the giant shark far more than it favors you. It uses that room immediately, circling wide before committing to a rush that comes from an angle you did not expect. You pivot hard, catching your balance against the drag of the current, and meet its charge with steel raised just as the impact sends both of you spinning through the murky water.

The sunken temple holds its cold silence like a living thing, broken only by the steady drip of ancient water finding new paths through cracked stone somewhere far above your head. You move through the gloom carefully, weapon ready, every sense straining for the first sign of the giant shark's approach through the murk. It comes without warning, a sudden surge of muscle and teeth from a curtain of silt you had not thought to watch closely enough. You twist hard, feeling the current drag violently at your armor, and answer the passing rush with a fast, desperate strike before it can circle back around.

You press deeper into the flooded ruin, past columns wrapped thick in pale moss that provides the only real light in this drowned world of crushing dark. The water here runs colder, and somewhere ahead the current shifts strange, curling back on itself in a way that tells you the giant shark has already noticed your approach. You brace against a broken pillar, weapon raised, and wait for the moment it finally commits to the attack rather than continuing to circle. When it comes, jaws wide and fast, you meet the charge without flinching, and the impact staggers you both through billowing clouds of disturbed silt.

The drowned hall shudders as something enormous slams a fallen column somewhere ahead of you, testing the water for a reaction it clearly expects to draw out. You hold your ground instead, weapon ready, refusing to give away your exact position through careless movement in the murky dark. The giant shark grows impatient after a long moment of silence, finally surging into the open water with jaws already wide and closing fast. You meet the charge head on, bracing hard against the impact, and your blade finds its mark just as the churning current threatens to pull you both apart entirely.

Past the broken archway, the temple opens into a long, flooded corridor lined with statues worn faceless by centuries of relentless current. You move through carefully, weapon ready, watching the shifting shadows for any hint of the giant shark's massive shape somewhere close in the crushing dark ahead. It finds you near the midpoint, surging from behind a fallen statue with a speed that closes the gap before you can fully brace. You twist hard against the current, feeling your boots skid on ancient tile, and drive your weapon up into its exposed flank as it passes close enough to touch you.

The cold water presses hard against your chest as you descend further into the ruin, each step taking you deeper into a darkness that feels increasingly deliberate, increasingly watched. Somewhere ahead, past a curtain of drifting silt, the giant shark waits with a patience that unsettles you more than any obvious aggression could. You press forward anyway, weapon leading the way, refusing to let the waiting game favor something so much larger than yourself. When it finally breaks cover, jaws wide and closing fast, you meet the rush without hesitation, driving your blade home just as the impact sends both of you reeling.

The sunken temple's deepest hall holds a stillness that feels almost sacred, broken only by the faint glow of scattered bioluminescent moss clinging to broken pillars. You move through it carefully, weapon ready, aware that the giant shark you have been tracking through the ruin has likely already found you first. The confirmation comes fast, a sudden surge from the gloom that closes the distance before you can fully react to the sound. You throw yourself sideways hard, feeling the rush of displaced water drag at your armor, and answer its passing charge with a fast, desperate strike of your own making.

Ahead, the corridor bends sharply around a collapsed section of ceiling, forcing you to pick your way carefully over scattered rubble in the crushing dark. Something vast stirs the silt just beyond the bend, and you know before you round it that the giant shark has already chosen this as its ground. It confirms your suspicion the moment you clear the rubble, surging forward with jaws wide and closing distance faster than you expected. You brace hard against a fallen block of stone, meet the charge head on, and feel the full weight of the impact travel through your arms as your blade finds purchase.

The drowned hall stretches wide here, its far end lost in gloom too thick for even the moss to light properly, and the open space unsettles you more than any narrow corridor could. You move carefully, weapon ready, watching every shifting shadow for the telltale shape of the giant shark closing in from somewhere unseen. It comes from an angle you did not expect, cutting across the open water with a speed that leaves little room to react properly. You pivot hard, catching your balance against the drag of the current, and meet its charge with steel raised just as the impact sends silt billowing everywhere around you.

You have tracked the giant shark's scars across three broken pillars now, each mark deeper than the last, evidence of a hunter that has fought and won here many times before your own arrival. Somewhere close in the gloom ahead, it circles patiently, waiting for you to make the first careless mistake it needs. You refuse to give it the satisfaction, holding your ground firm against the pull of the current until it finally loses patience and commits to the charge. The impact staggers you hard when it finally comes, but your blade finds its mark in the same instant, and steaming blood clouds the water between you.

The temple's crushing dark presses in from every direction as you press deeper toward the sunken altar, cold water finding every gap in your armor despite your best efforts to seal them. Somewhere ahead, the giant shark waits with a patience that feels almost deliberate, almost designed to wear down your nerve before the fight even begins. It breaks that patience suddenly, surging from behind a broken statue with jaws already wide and closing fast through the murky water. You twist hard against the current, feeling your boots skid on centuries of accumulated silt, and answer its rush with a strike aimed for the softer flesh behind its jaw.

Past the last of the broken columns, the drowned hall opens into the sunken altar chamber itself, its centerpiece worn smooth by centuries of slow, relentless current. You approach carefully, weapon ready, certain that the giant shark you have been tracking has already claimed this space as its final ground. The confirmation comes immediately, a sudden surge from behind the altar itself that closes the distance faster than you thought possible in water this cold. You throw yourself sideways hard, feeling the rush of displaced current drag at your armor, and meet its passing charge with a fast, sure strike of your own.

The water here holds an unnatural stillness that you have learned to distrust more than any obvious threat in the crushing dark of this drowned ruin. You raise your weapon, plant your feet firm in the silt below, and wait patiently for the giant shark to finally reveal itself from wherever it has been watching you. A drowned bell hangs silent overhead, coral crusted and forgotten by everyone but the patient water itself. Beneath it waits the wavebender, arms open in something almost like welcome, current already rising to meet your very first careful step forward into the chamber. The whole hall seems deliberately built around this one moment of arrival, every drowned stone angled just so. You hesitate at the threshold, feeling the pressure change before the current even truly does. A slow, deliberate pull tugs hard at your boots, testing your balance the way water tests every weakness it finds in stone or in flesh. The wavebender watches calmly, letting the temple soften you first before she moves at all.

The passage floods your lungs with pressure and biting cold long before you finally reach the coral altar at its end. Ahead, blue light traces a crowned silhouette against the crushing dark, entirely unmoving in a way that somehow unsettles far more than motion ever would. You realize slowly that she has simply been waiting this whole time for you to notice her first. When recognition finally passes between you, the wavebender raises one webbed hand and the current answers instantly, curling tight around broken columns like it has always known exactly where this moment was heading. You grip your weapon tighter, understanding now that patience like hers is its own kind of terrible weapon.

You step carefully over a fallen spear, rusted clean through and abandoned in the ancient silt. Whoever carried it met the wavebender before you did, and clearly did not carry it back out of this drowned place again. The current around you tightens now, patient and unhurried, as though it has all the time in the world left to finish exactly what it started here centuries ago. Ahead, the wavebender waits at the coral altar, crown dripping steady blue light onto the silt below her bare feet. She does not acknowledge the spear, or you, not yet. She simply lets the current do the announcing, curling tighter around your ankles with every careful step you take toward her.

The temple's deepest hall glows faint blue, lit by something quietly, patiently alive within the coral itself. At its heart stands the wavebender, current curling around her shoulders like an old and loyal pet finally, happily returning home after a very long absence. She watches you cross the flooded threshold without so much as raising a single hand in greeting or warning. You approach slowly, boots dragging against silt that seems reluctant to let you pass at all. When you're close enough to see her eyes clearly in the blue light, she finally moves, current snapping tight around your legs like a trap that has been patiently set for longer than you can possibly imagine.

A ripple crosses the vaulted ceiling above you, wrong in a way you can't quite name, far too deliberate to be ordinary current alone. You look down just as the water beneath your feet tightens its grip without warning. The wavebender has already decided, calmly and completely, exactly where you're meant to stand for whatever comes next in this drowned and ancient place. She emerges from the gloom ahead, crown catching faint light, current coiling tight around one wrist like a weapon she has not yet chosen to draw. You brace yourself against the pull, understanding that this fight will happen entirely on her terms, in her temple, in water that answers only to her.

The corridor bends toward a soft blue glow that seems to breathe in and out along with the current itself. Something large moves just beyond the light, unhurried, testing the water carefully before it finally commits to anything at all. When it steps forward at last, the tide bends first, rushing ahead of her like an eager and loyal thing, and only afterward does the wavebender herself follow. You raise your weapon, feeling the current tighten around your calves in response. She studies you for a long moment, crown dripping, before finally speaking through the water itself rather than her mouth: a slow, deliberate pull that says, clearer than words, that she has already chosen how this ends.

The last doorway opens onto black water lit by slow, drifting motes of light like dying stars scattered across the dark. At its center a crowned shape turns toward you, current gathering at her fingertips like a breath about to be released all at once into the surrounding water. The whole drowned world seems to lean toward that single held moment, waiting. You step through the doorway carefully, feeling pressure build behind your eyes with every stride. The wavebender watches your approach without moving, patient as only something this old can afford to be, letting the current test your balance long before she ever needs to test your strength directly herself.

A current coils lazily around a fallen sword, dragging it slowly across the silt floor like a curious hand testing an old, forgotten toy. You look up sharply, heart hammering hard against your ribs in the sudden quiet. The wavebender is already closer than she was only moments ago, and you never once saw her actually move so much as an inch in the drowned gloom. She studies you calmly from across the chamber, crown catching the last of your failing torchlight. Current spools from her open fingers like thread pulled from a loom that never once stops turning, not even for a moment. You realize, too late perhaps, that the fight already began the instant you first stepped through her doorway.

The altar chamber hums with a low, ancient song that only the water itself seems to remember clearly after all these centuries. The wavebender waits at its center, crown dripping steady light onto silt gone thick with age, current already coiling tight for whatever comes first between the two of you. You get the distinct sense she has performed this exact moment more times than memory could ever hold. You approach across the flooded floor, boots dragging against resistance that shouldn't exist in ordinary water like this. She raises one hand slowly, and the ancient song shifts pitch, current answering her gesture like it has been trained to for longer than your own people have even existed.

You duck beneath a drowned lintel and feel the water tighten sharply around your legs, testing your resolve before you've even fully entered the chamber. Ahead, a pale figure raises one webbed hand, unhurried, and the whole ancient temple seems to lean in closer to watch exactly what happens next between the two of you. Coral thrones sit empty behind her, patient as she is, waiting for nothing in particular anymore. The wavebender lowers her hand slowly, and the current surges forward to meet you, cold and insistent against your legs. You steady yourself against the pull, gripping your weapon tighter as the drowned hall grows suddenly, unmistakably quiet around the both of you.

Bioluminescence flickers along a fallen archway, tracing a narrow and deliberate path through gloom gone thick with silt disturbed by something unseen. At its end waits the wavebender, current curling around her shoulders like a cloak worn without any visible effort at all on her part. She has clearly stood in that exact spot far longer than the fragile, flickering light suggests to you now. You approach across broken stone, feeling pressure ache steadily behind your eyes with every careful step forward. When you're finally close enough to see her clearly, she raises one hand and the current answers at once, curling tight and cold around your ankles like a trap sprung after a very long and patient wait.

The dark presses close as the corridor bends steadily, relentlessly downward, swallowing your torchlight a little more with every single turn you take. A pulse of blue answers from somewhere below, rhythmic and patient in a way that ordinary current never quite manages on its own. Something ancient rises slowly from the drowned floor ahead of you, entirely unhurried, to finally meet you here. The wavebender steps forward from the settling silt, webbed hand already tracing slow, deliberate circles through the surrounding current. Water bends instantly to her will, curling tight around broken columns like it remembers every single order she has ever given it across the centuries.

A soundless wave rolls through the flooded hall ahead of you, bending the water sideways hard enough to stagger your footing against the cold, unforgiving stone. You brace with both hands, torch nearly guttering out completely in the sudden turbulence that follows. Something crowned and infinitely patient waits just beyond the disturbance, content to let the ancient temple do the softening work entirely for her. When the water finally stills around you, you see her clearly for the very first time: the wavebender, arms loose, current curling obediently around her wrist like a loyal animal that has finally, happily returned home to her side.

Cold water floods your boots as the ancient floor gives slightly beneath your weight, unstable after so many centuries spent beneath the crushing sea. Somewhere close a current hums with plain, unmistakable intent, curling around broken columns like it's searching methodically for a way inside your defenses. The crowned shape watching from the surrounding dark has not blinked once since you first entered this drowned place. You press forward anyway, torch trembling slightly in your grip. The wavebender finally steps into full view, current already spooling from her fingers, patient and utterly certain of exactly how this particular fight is going to end for you.

Pressure aches steadily behind your eyes as the ancient temple deepens around you, dark pressing in from every side like something almost alive and breathing. A pale shape glides along the vaulted ceiling above, trailing threads of light that never quite touch the black water waiting below. It watches you the way a patient tide studies a slowly shrinking shore, unhurried and utterly certain. When you finally reach the flooded altar chamber, the wavebender is already there waiting, crown catching the last of your failing torchlight. Current curls tight around her wrist, coiled and ready, while she studies your approach as though every single step had already been written somewhere long before you arrived.

Coral crunches underfoot as you edge carefully past a broken statue lying face down in centuries of undisturbed silt. The water thickens around you with every step, resisting like it has a will entirely its own, testing your resolve before the real fight even begins. Ahead, a crowned shape finally turns to face you directly, patient and utterly unafraid of whatever weapon you carry. Current already spools from her open fingers like thread pulled from a loom that never once stops turning for anyone. The wavebender studies you a long moment before finally speaking through the water itself, a slow pull at your ankles that says, clearer than words ever could, exactly how patient she has learned to be.

You press through a curtain of dense kelp into open, black water thick with silt and the memory of centuries. A low hum vibrates through the current itself, unmistakably deliberate, nothing like the ordinary shifting tides you've felt before in shallower places. Ahead, webbed hands part the water like curtains being drawn back slowly, revealing a patient crown beneath. The wavebender steps forward into the dim blue light, current curling obediently around her shoulders like a cloak she has worn for longer than memory holds. You raise your weapon, feeling the pressure build steadily behind your eyes as the ancient chamber grows quiet around the both of you, waiting.

The corridor floods faster than you ever expected, cold biting hard through every seam of your worn gear as you push deeper. Ahead, a shape glides calmly against the current instead of with it, patient while you struggle openly to keep pace with her unhurried movement. Somewhere behind her, drowned banners drift overhead, torn and long since colorless in the crushing dark. She finally turns to face you fully, crown dripping faint light onto silt gone thick with age and neglect. The wavebender raises one hand slowly, and the current answers at once, curling tight and cold around your legs like a trap that has been patiently, carefully set for you alone.

The corridor drops away into black water, and the current pulls you forward before your torch even gutters out. Somewhere ahead something enormous shifts against stone, a groan like a hull under strain, and bioluminescent moss along the drowned columns flares blue in answer. This is the deepest hall of the temple, the one the maps refused to draw, and the dragon turtle that guards it has not seen sunlight in a thousand years. It rises from the silt in sections, shell first, wide as a longship and crusted with barnacles and old gold leaf that has not worn off. Two eyes the size of shields fix on you, and the water itself seems to thicken with its attention. Roll for initiative; a dragon turtle this old does not need to be fast to be first. You go in low, blade angled for the seam where throat meets shell, because the plates up top will turn anything short of a critical hit. The first exchange is a test: it wants to see what you are made of before it wastes its breath weapon. Answer honestly. Answer with steel. By the third exchange the shell's rhythm becomes plain: it favors its right flank when it turns, dragging that hind leg a beat slower than the rest, and a sharp eye can use that lag to slip past the jaw. The steam breath, when it finally comes, boils the water into a blinding curtain for a two count. Ride it out behind the column, then push in while the ancient creature is still recovering its wind.

Bioluminescent lichen lines the sunken hall in cold blue veins, enough light to fight by and just enough to see the shape rising from the collapsed roof: a shell wider than the room, golden scutes dulled by silt, a jaw that could close a portcullis. The dragon turtle has slept here since before the temple drowned, and your torch smoke curling upward through water is the rudest thing to happen to it in years. It does not charge. It settles instead, forelimbs planting into the seabed, and a sound builds in its chest like a kettle nearing boil. That is the tell for the breath weapon, a gout of superheated steam that will cook anyone caught in the open. Scatter behind the fallen columns; the stone is old but it still blocks scalding water. When the steam passes, close the distance fast. Its underside near the joints is the only soft place on that whole armored bulk, and a well aimed strike there does real damage while the rest of your hits glance off shell like coins off a countertop. The second gout of steam comes sooner than the first, a sign it is growing irritated rather than merely cautious, and the fog it leaves behind stings even from a distance. Push through anyway. A dragon turtle that keeps venting breath weapon back to back is a dragon turtle running low on the reserve that fuels it, and that reserve, once spent, takes long minutes underwater to rebuild.

The current changes before you see anything, a slow displaced surge of water pushing back against you, and that is how you know the dragon turtle has turned to face the party. Its shell scrapes the temple roof, shedding a rain of loosened stone, and for a moment the whole ruined hall rumbles like it might come down on everyone at once. Fifteen feet of ancient reptile does not need to be quick to be dangerous. It closes the gap in two great strokes of its legs, jaw snapping shut a handspan from where a fighter stood a heartbeat earlier, and the shove of displaced water alone nearly knocks the party off their feet. Brace against the nearest column and get your dice ready. The fight settles into a rhythm: dodge the jaw, wait out the steam breath, dart in when its head turns to track someone else. Every hit that lands on the shell is wasted effort; every hit that finds the throat or the tendon behind a foreleg counts double. Aim carefully. This is not a fight you win by swinging harder. Somewhere in the chaos a shove of displaced current knocks the party's smallest member sideways into a fallen column, and for a heartbeat it looks like the fight might end there. It does not. Recovering fast, everyone regroups behind the stone, waits out the next gout of steam, and returns to the plan: strike narrow, strike the seams, and let the shell take every blow meant to miss.

Something ancient wakes in the deepest chamber of the sunken temple, and the whole drowned hall seems to hold its breath with you. The dragon turtle uncurls from where it has rested for a millennium, shell scraping free of silt in a slow grinding whoosh, and its eyes catch the bioluminescent glow like twin captured moons. It does not roar at first. It simply rises, water displaced in a widening ring, until its head is level with the broken altar at the room's center. Then the roar comes, a physical thing that rattles your teeth and sends a shockwave of bubbles boiling off its jaw. Initiative is rolled in that instant, and the fight begins whether you are ready or not. Its favored trick is patience: let you spend your best attacks on the shell, let the current carry your strength away, and wait for an opening its steam breath can exploit. Do not give it that opening. Strike the seams, not the plates, and keep one eye on the gout of scalding water always building in its throat. Twice more the gout of scalding water rolls through the chamber, and twice more the party answers with narrow strikes to the throat while its guard is wide. By the third exchange the ancient thing is finally slowing, breath ragged, shell dragging low against the silt. That slowdown is the whole fight in miniature: wear down patience with patience, until even a thousand year old guardian runs out of both.

Cold water presses in from every side as you cross the last submerged threshold, and the temple opens into a vast collapsed nave where a single shaft of surface light somehow still finds its way down. In the middle of that pale column rests the dragon turtle, so still you could mistake it for a boulder until one golden eye opens. It has guarded this place since before the roof caved in, and the coins scattered across the floor, the columns of gold leaf still fixed to fallen stone, all of it belongs to a hoard it has never once had to defend. Today it will. Draw steel and step into the light; the current carries the sound of your approach straight to it. The turtle uncoils in a single motion, shell first, then the long neck, then a jaw wide enough to swallow a shield whole. Its breath weapon vents first, a boiling gout that turns the water white, and only after that does it close for the bite. Survive the steam. The bite is the easier problem. Somewhere past the second exchange the dragon turtle changes tactics, dragging its bulk sideways to pin someone against a fallen column instead of simply biting. It is a clumsy trick from a creature built for straightforward violence, and it costs the beast a full turn to set up, a turn spent not breathing steam and not guarding its throat. Take it.

The dragon turtle's shell alone is the size of a longship's hull, and when it turns broadside in the drowned hall you understand exactly why the old maps marked this chamber as impassable. Barnacles and drowned gold leaf crust its back; underneath, its belly plates are pale and soft, the only place a blade will actually bite. It attacks in a set rhythm you learn fast or not at all: a slamming forelimb, a pause while it gathers breath, then the steam gout that fills the chamber with scalding fog. Time your dodge to that pause. Everyone who waits half a second too long gets cooked instead of merely singed, and half a second is all the warning you get down here. Between gouts of steam, close in low and fast, going for the tendon behind a foreleg or the soft seam beneath the jaw. Every clean hit there staggers it enough to buy the next exchange. It is old, and slow to turn, and that slowness is the only edge you have against something this size. Its bite finally lands somewhere in the third exchange, a glancing hit that still nearly takes a shield arm off, proof enough that even a slow ancient thing can be dangerous when it finally connects. Answer immediately. A staggered dragon turtle that has just spent its bite is briefly wide open at the throat, and that opening does not last more than a heartbeat.

Deep beneath the drowned nave, past columns furred green with age, the water goes still in a way that feels deliberate, like something enormous is holding its breath to listen. Then the dragon turtle surfaces from the silt floor, shell first, displacing enough water to rock you back a step before it has even moved to attack. Its eyes are old and yellow and utterly unbothered by your torchlight. It has eaten fools before who mistook its stillness for sleep, and the crushed remains of at least one previous party still rot near the base of the altar it guards. Initiative rolls; the current itself seems to lean in to watch. You open with a feint toward the shell, drawing its attention while a second strike goes for the throat seam instead. It works, barely, a shallow cut that draws no blood but proves the plan sound. Keep drawing its guard wide and keep striking narrow. That is the only shape this fight can take and still end in your favor. The rhythm holds for three full exchanges: steam, cover, close, retreat. By the fourth, the shell is dragging visibly in the silt, and the gouts of scalding water come slower, less certain, more like the last gasps of a furnace running out of fuel than the confident opening breath from before. Push the advantage now, while it still can be pushed.

A wall of scalding water slams into the chamber before you even see the dragon turtle move, steam boiling off its jaw and rolling across the drowned floor like a living thing. Whoever is caught in the open takes damage before they even understand what happened; the smart move was already ducking behind a column two seconds ago. When the fog clears, the turtle has closed half the distance, forelimbs planting into silt with enough force to shake loose stone from the collapsed roof above. Its shell shrugs off a first strike without so much as a scratch, gold leaf and barnacle alike untouched. The message is clear: nothing about that shell is going to give. So go under it instead. The soft plates near the joints, the pale seam beneath its throat, those are the only real targets in this fight. Every hit that lands there staggers the beast; every hit that lands anywhere else just wastes a turn you cannot spare against something this patient and this old. A second gout follows faster than expected, catching someone still recovering from the first, and the burn of it lingers long after the steam clears. Grit through it. The soft plates near the joints are softer now, the whole creature visibly tiring, and every strike that finds that seam counts for more than it did at the fight's opening moments.

The temple's deepest hall is a drowned throne room, broken columns rising into darkness overhead, and at its center sits a dragon turtle so large it has reshaped the silt around itself into a shallow crater. Coins and gold leaf litter that crater like an offering, decades of unlucky visitors who never made it back out. It watches you cross the threshold without moving, patient in the way only a truly ancient thing can be patient, and only when you are close enough for the fight to matter does it finally rise. Water surges outward from the motion, a shove strong enough to stagger anyone standing too near, and its jaw opens on a rumble that promises worse. The steam breath comes first, always first, a gout of boiling water that fills the chamber white. Survive that, then close the distance while it gathers itself for the next one. The rhythm is brutal but learnable: dodge, close, strike the seam, retreat. Four beats, repeated, until the ancient thing finally goes still. Twice the beast tries to pin the party against the crater's rim, forelimbs slamming down hard enough to crack more of the collapsed roof loose above. Twice the trick fails, dodged by inches, and each failed lunge costs the ancient thing a turn it cannot spend guarding the seam beneath its jaw. Every wasted turn on its part is a turn earned on yours.

Somewhere above, sunlight tries and fails to reach the bottom of this drowned nave, and the only real light comes from lichen glowing faint blue along the fallen columns. That is enough to see the dragon turtle when it finally moves, shell grinding free of a thousand years of silt, eyes opening like lamps behind old glass. It has guarded this hoard since the temple first went under, back when the roof was still a roof and the altar still held its offerings upright. None of that matters to it now; only the intrusion matters, and the intrusion is you. It rises with a roar that sends a visible shockwave rippling through the water between you. The fight that follows is a lesson in patience. Its bite is fast, its steam breath faster, but its turns are slow, and slow turns mean you can circle behind it if you are quick and lucky. Go for the tendon at the back of a foreleg first. A staggered dragon turtle is a turtle that finally stops being unkillable. Its roar echoes twice more through the ruined nave as the fight wears on, each one a little less certain than the last, the water shoving outward with a little less force. That fading confidence is the surest sign the plan is working: circle wide, strike narrow, and let the ancient thing's own bulk work against it in these tight collapsed halls.

The current tastes different this deep, mineral and cold, and it carries the first warning of the dragon turtle before your eyes ever find it: a low pulsing thrum, water shoved rhythmically by something breathing far larger than any breath you know. You round the last fallen column and there it is, shell wide as the collapsed roof above it. It does not attack immediately. It studies you instead, ancient reflexes weighing whether you are worth the effort of rising fully from the silt. Then, apparently deciding you are, it does rise, and the shove of displaced water alone is enough to knock a smaller party member clean off their feet. Steam comes next, a boiling exhale that turns the water in front of it opaque and white. Get clear of that cone or take real damage. Once it passes, the turtle's head is briefly lowered and exposed, jaw still steaming, and that half second is the best opening you will get to drive a blade into the soft seam beneath its throat. The half second window after the steam gout becomes the whole rhythm of the fight, repeated again and again until it feels almost familiar: brace, breathe, strike, retreat. By the fourth repetition the throat seam is raw and the ancient creature's guard finally starts dropping wide, its head sagging lower each time it exhales.

Every column in this drowned hall has been felled at least once, snapped clean through and left where they fell, and it takes you a moment standing in that ruin to understand what did the felling. The dragon turtle sleeping at the chamber's heart is large enough to have done it simply by rolling over in its sleep. It is not asleep now. Your torchlight woke it three columns back, and by the time you reach the open floor its golden eyes are already tracking you, jaw working slightly as if tasting the water for your scent. When it finally moves, it moves all at once, a lunge that closes ten feet in a single stroke. The shell takes every early hit without complaint, barnacles and gold leaf both untouched by steel. The soft plates near its joints are the answer, along with the pale seam under its jaw where a well placed strike actually draws blood. Keep circling. Keep striking narrow. The old thing tires slower than you do, but it does tire. By the second lunge its patience is visibly thinning, jaw snapping at empty water where a fighter stood a half second earlier, frustration plain in the force of the miss. Frustrated things make mistakes. The next opening at the throat comes wider and lasts longer than the last, and it is answered with everything the party has left to give.

Bubbles rise past you in a steady silver column, oxygen from somewhere far above leaking down through a crack in the drowned roof, and it is that faint light glinting off gold that first reveals the dragon turtle's hoard scattered across the chamber floor. The turtle itself is harder to see until it moves, shell colored exactly like the silt it rests in. When it does move, the whole floor seems to shift with it, ancient muscle rolling beneath armor thick enough to have turned aside a thousand years of cave ins. It fixes both eyes on you and opens its jaw wide enough to swallow a shield, and the sound that follows is less a roar than a physical shove of water. Get behind cover before the steam breath follows. Once it passes, close in fast; its head lowers to breathe again only a few seconds later, and that lowered angle briefly exposes the soft seam under its jaw. Strike there, retreat, repeat. Patience wins this fight faster than fury ever will. A silver column of bubbles from a lucky sword strike marks the first real wound, a thin dark thread rising from the soft seam beneath its throat. It is not much, not yet, but it is proof the plan works, and proof enough to keep pressing while the ancient guardian's confidence, so absolute a few exchanges ago, visibly starts to crack.

The drowned nave narrows toward its far end into a single collapsed archway, half blocked by rubble from the roof that gave way centuries ago, and it is through that narrow gap that you first glimpse the dragon turtle's eye, huge and golden and entirely too aware of you already. It does not fit through the archway, which is the only reason you have time to plan before the fight starts in earnest. When you finally step past the rubble into the open chamber, it is already turning to meet you, shell scraping stone, gold leaf and old coin sliding off its back in a small avalanche. The steam breath opens the exchange, as it always does with this creature, boiling the water white for a long two count. Behind cover, count those two seconds and move the instant they end. Its head dips low right after venting, jaw still smoking, and that lowered angle is your best and briefest chance to strike the soft seam beneath it. Every strike that lands on gold plated shell rings out through the water like a hammer on an anvil, useless noise that accomplishes nothing but drawing its attention. Ignore that instinct to swing at the loudest target. The seam beneath the jaw, quieter and far less satisfying to hit, is the only place tonight's fight is actually being decided.

Something enormous has been resting against the temple's deepest wall long enough for barnacles to grow across half its shell, and it is only when that shell shifts, slow and grinding, that you realize the wall itself is alive. The dragon turtle peels away from the stone it has slept against for a thousand years, gold leaf and silt sliding off in sheets. Its eyes find you before its body has even fully turned, old and yellow and unblinking, weighing exactly how much trouble you represent. Not enough, apparently, to make it hurry; it rises at its own pace, jaw opening on a low rumble that builds steadily toward the roar that will start this fight in earnest. When the roar comes, so does the shockwave, a physical shove of water that rattles the fallen columns around you. Brace, then close the moment its steam breath passes. The soft seam beneath its jaw is the only honest target on that whole armored bulk, and every strike anywhere else is steel wasted against gold plated stone. Fourteen feet of ancient reptile does not tire quickly, but it does tire, and by the fight's middle stretch its turns come visibly slower, its steam gouts spaced further apart. Keep the rhythm steady: dodge, close, strike the seam, retreat. Patience is the only weapon here that never runs dry, not even against something a thousand years old.

A single shaft of surface light finds its way down through a crack in the drowned roof, illuminating a slow drift of silt across the chamber floor, and it is in that pale column that the dragon turtle first reveals itself, rising from the silt like the temple's own broken foundation come to life. It is old beyond guessing, its shell a patchwork of gold leaf and barnacle and scars from fights long since forgotten by everyone but itself. It studies you the way a landlord studies an uninvited guest, unhurried, faintly annoyed, entirely unafraid. Then it decides you are worth the effort and the fight begins for real. Steam comes first, boiling the water into blinding fog; get clear or take the damage. When the fog thins, its head is lowered from the exhale, jaw still smoking faintly, and that half second of exposure is the whole plan: strike the seam beneath the jaw, retreat before the bite answers, and wait out the next gout of scalding breath. The old thing's jaw finally catches a glancing hit near the fight's midpoint, snapping shut on nothing but water where a party member stood a heartbeat before, and the frustration in that miss is almost audible. Frustration is useful. It means the next opening at the throat will come wider, angrier, and easier to exploit than anything offered so far.

Deep water presses against your ears like a hand as you descend the last flooded stair, and the pressure only grows worse when the dragon turtle finally reveals itself at the bottom, a shape so large it takes a moment to register as a single living creature rather than a fallen section of temple wall. It has claimed this chamber for longer than anyone alive can measure, and the scattered coins and cracked columns around its resting place are proof enough of how many have tried and failed to take it from here. It rises slowly, shell scraping free of silt, eyes opening like twin lanterns behind old glass. The fight opens with its steam breath, a boiling exhale that fills the chamber white and forces everyone behind whatever cover the collapsed columns still offer. Once it passes, close fast; the seam beneath its jaw is soft where the rest of it is stone. Strike there, retreat, and let its own slow patience work against it. Twice the party circles wide to avoid a fresh gout of steam, and twice the maneuver costs the ancient guardian more than it costs them, each wasted breath leaving its throat exposed a beat longer than the last. By the time the shell finally drags low in the silt, worn down rather than broken, the whole rhythm of the fight has become second nature.

The temple's deepest hall smells of salt and old copper, and the current here moves in slow deliberate pulses, almost like breathing. That is because something is breathing: the dragon turtle, half buried in silt at the chamber's center, chest rising and falling in a rhythm large enough to stir the whole floor. It wakes the instant your torchlight crosses its eye, and there is no groggy pause, no slow uncurling. One moment it is still; the next its shell is turning to face you fully, gold leaf sliding free in sheets, and a rumble building in its throat that you have already learned to fear. The steam gout that follows fills the chamber with blinding heat. Get clear, then close while its head is still lowered from the exhale. The seam beneath its jaw is the only soft place on an otherwise impenetrable creature, and every clean strike there buys the party a little more room to survive what comes next. The next gout of steam catches the chamber at an angle, rolling low across the floor instead of straight ahead, and dodging it means diving rather than simply ducking behind stone. It works, barely, and the moment of scrambling recovery afterward almost costs someone a hit from the follow up bite. Stay sharp. This creature adapts as the fight wears on.

Fallen columns litter the drowned hall like felled trees, evidence of a fight that happened here long before you arrived, and it is threading between them that you first catch sight of the dragon turtle's shell, gold leaf dulled by centuries but unmistakably still catching what little light reaches this deep. It is resting, or pretending to, one golden eye cracked open just enough to track your approach. The moment you are close enough to matter, that pretense ends; the shell turns, water shoves outward in a heavy ring, and a jaw wide enough to take a shield whole opens on a rumble that fills the whole ruined nave. Steam comes next, scalding and blinding, forcing the party behind whatever column still stands. When it clears, strike fast and low, aiming for the soft seam beneath the jaw rather than wasting steel on shell that has turned aside worse blows than yours for a thousand years running. By the fight's later stretch the roar comes weaker, more habit than threat, and the shockwave it once sent through the water barely stirs the silt underfoot anymore. That fading power says everything: the ancient guardian is running out of whatever fuels its breath weapon, and every strike still landing on the throat seam brings that end closer.

You feel the dragon turtle before you see it, a slow heavy pulse of displaced water moving through the drowned nave like a second current layered over the first. Then the silt ahead shifts and heaves, and a shape the size of a longship rises out of it, shell crusted gold and barnacle both. It has clearly been resting here a long while; coins and cracked columns lie undisturbed around its resting spot, a small kingdom it has never had to actually defend. Today changes that. It fixes both eyes on the party and rises fully, jaw opening on a rumble that builds toward a roar loud enough to rattle stone. The steam breath that follows the roar is the real danger, boiling the water white for a long count before it fades. Get behind cover, then close the instant it clears; the soft seam under its jaw, briefly exposed while its head stays lowered, is the only place a blade will do real damage tonight. A second wave of steam catches the party mid regroup, forcing an ugly scramble back behind the columns with burns to show for it. Push through the sting anyway. Its head stays lowered a beat longer after this exhale than after the first, tired rather than cautious now, and that extra beat is worth more than the burn cost to earn it.

The archway into the temple's final chamber has partly collapsed, forcing you to duck through a gap barely wide enough for a shield, and on the far side the ceiling opens into a vast drowned nave where the dragon turtle waits, already awake, already watching the gap you just came through. It has clearly heard you coming for some time; its shell is already turned to face the archway, gold leaf and barnacle glinting faintly in the bioluminescent glow from the lichen along the walls. There is no surprise round here, no easy opening strike. Just the two of you, and the water between. It opens with its steam breath rather than waiting for you to close the distance, a boiling gout that fills the narrow chamber fast. Get clear, then push in while its head stays low from the exhale. The seam beneath its jaw is soft; everything else about this ancient creature is unforgiving stone. The party's rhythm holds through a second and third exchange, steam then cover then a fast strike at the throat, and each cycle leaves the ancient creature a little slower to recover than the one before. By the time its jaw finally hangs open, breath ragged, the fight has turned from desperate defense into something almost like a hunt.

Somewhere in the drowned dark ahead, something enormous exhales, and the sound travels through the water as a low physical thrum you feel in your teeth before you hear it. That is your only warning before the dragon turtle surfaces from the silt floor of the temple's last chamber, shell wide as a fallen roof beam. Its eyes are the color of old brass, dulled by a thousand years underwater but sharp enough to track every twitch of your approach. It has guarded this hoard since before the temple's roof gave way, and the coins scattered across the floor beneath it are proof no one has managed to take it from here yet. It rises fully with a roar, water shoving outward hard enough to rattle the nearest column loose from the ceiling above. Steam follows, boiling and blinding; survive that, then close in on the soft seam beneath its jaw while its head is still lowered from the breath. Everything else about it is armor. Its final gout of steam comes late and weak, more habit than real threat, and the shell that once shrugged off every blow now drags visibly low against the silt floor. One last strike to the soft seam beneath the jaw is all it takes to finish what a dozen exchanges of patience already decided. The ancient guardian goes still, and the hoard beneath it is finally, quietly, yours.
